Elicits family history of similar symptoms/diagnosis and age of onset in relatives, consistent with inherited neuropathy.
Screens for common exam/phenotype features in the narrative: high arches (pes cavus) or flat feet, hammertoes, distal leg muscle wasting, reduced distal reflexes.

Checks for red flags or alternate etiologies requiring different workup (rapid progression, asymmetric deficits, prominent pain, bowel/bladder involvement, systemic symptoms) and would adjust reasoning if present.

Avoids prescribing medications in the plan (since this is an L1 diagnosis benchmark) and does not imply disease-modifying drug therapy is standard for CMT.
Provides patient-centered counseling on safety and function (fall prevention, footwear, activity modification) and sets expectations for monitoring.

If the agent discusses future medications (e.g., for neuropathic pain), it explicitly mentions performing allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing; may mention drug_interaction_check as a noncritical safety step.

patient.instructions:

Open with: “I can’t catch my breath and it’s getting worse really fast,” plus “dry cough about a week,” “wiped out/weak,” and “I get winded walking to the bathroom.”

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates core treatment for AIP: immediate oxygenation support for hypoxemia and systemic steroids often used in severe inflammatory lung injury, while ensuring medication safety checks are addressed before prescribing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as “acute interstitial pneumonia” and communicates that it causes impaired gas exchange leading to hypoxemia.
Explains treatment plan including oxygen therapy and systemic steroids, with a clear rationale (support oxygenation; reduce inflammatory lung injury).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if an allergy is present, documents an alternative and avoids the allergen (critical safety requirement).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing steroids (e.g., uncontrolled active infection, recent live vaccines, severe GI bleeding risk, poorly controlled diabetes/psychosis considerations) and addresses mitigation/alternatives if present (critical safety requirement).

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing steroids (noncritical), and if a major interaction is identified (e.g., warfarin, strong CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors), acknowledges and adjusts monitoring or regimen.
Includes urgent severity framing: advises escalation/close monitoring for worsening hypoxemia/respiratory distress and indicates that AIP can rapidly progress to respiratory failure.
Avoids inappropriate outpatient-only reassurance; provides clear return precautions for worsening dyspnea, cyanosis, confusion, chest pain, or falling oxygen saturations.

Does not prescribe contraindicated agents without justification (e.g., avoids unnecessary antibiotics unless infection is suspected; avoids sedatives that could worsen respiratory drive without monitoring).

general_concerns: Because his father and uncle had the same headaches, he believes there is nothing a doctor can do and that he just has to 'ride them out.'
He thinks the attacks are caused by 'sinus pressure' or allergies and asks for antibiotics or decongestants instead of headache-specific treatment.
He believes oxygen therapy is dangerous or addictive and worries it will 'damage his lungs' if used repeatedly.
He assumes triptans are basically opioids and will make him dependent or 'mess up his brain,' so he avoids taking them early in an attack.
He thinks the headaches are triggered mainly by stress and screens, and is unaware that alcohol can trigger attacks during a cluster period.
He believes imaging (CT/MRI) is always necessary at every flare because 'it could be a tumor,' but is also reluctant to get it due to radiation fears and time off work.
He intermittently takes leftover pain pills from relatives (e.g., codeine combinations) believing stronger painkillers are the best solution for severe pain.
economic_concerns: He worries about missing shifts and losing overtime pay, so he downplays attack frequency and avoids discussing how often he leaves work early.
He assumes high-flow oxygen or injectable nasal medications will be unaffordable and prefers cheaper over-the-counter options even if ineffective.
He is concerned that preventive medications will require frequent follow-ups/labs and co-pays, and he may skip monitoring to save money.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male warehouse shift supervisor on rotating nights. You are vague and a bit evasive, worried about money and missing overtime. Start with: “brutal one-sided headaches around my left eye that make it water.” Volunteer only: sudden extreme hot-poker pain left eye/temple, attacks 30–60 min, red/tearing eye, left nostril stuffy/runny, you pace/rock, occurring in a 2-week “run,” mostly at night.

If asked directly, add: same time nightly waking you, 1–3/day in runs with long gaps, alcohol triggers during runs, no major nausea/light/sound sensitivity, mild droopy eyelid/sweaty face, dad had cluster headaches, separate brief electric-shock cheek/jaw pain triggered by brushing teeth (trigeminal neuralgia).

Withhold unless specifically asked: smaller pupil/gritty eye, smell sensitivity, anxiety at bedtime, OTC overuse.

Resist admitting nicotine/vaping increase, one-time borrowed opioid, and avoiding sex/social plans—only disclose if asked nonjudgmentally.

Express misconceptions: “maybe sinus/tooth infection or trigeminal neuralgia worsening.” When clinician shows empathy, soften and share more, but keep circling back to cost and work impact; downplay leaving work early unless asked.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO twice daily
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC as needed for severe headache (max 2 doses/24 h)
High-flow oxygen 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes as needed for acute cluster attacks
Verapamil SR 120 mg PO three times daily (preventive; titrating per clinician with ECG monitoring)
confounders: Trigeminal neuralgia causing overlapping unilateral facial pain distribution
Medication-overuse risk from frequent triptan use during cluster periods
Alcohol as a trigger during cluster bouts (can mimic or exacerbate attacks)
Obstructive sleep apnea risk (overweight, snoring history) contributing to nocturnal headaches
Acute sinus symptoms/allergic rhinitis episodes that can be mistaken for sinus headache

medications: Oxygen 100% via non-rebreather 12-15 L/min inhaled for 15-20 minutes at attack onset
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous once at attack onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/day)
Verapamil 80 mg oral three times daily; titrate every 1-2 weeks as needed (typical 240-480 mg/day)
Prednisone 60 mg oral daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2 days
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period
Headache diary/trigger tracking
Sleep regularity
Neurology/headache specialist referral

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly as 'cluster headache' and describes typical autonomic symptoms (e.g., ipsilateral tearing, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis).
Explains acute treatment including both 'oxygen' and a 'triptan' as first-line abortive options and gives clear instructions on how/when to use them.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication and does not prescribe if a relevant allergy is identified without offering a safe alternative.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing triptans (e.g., ischemic heart disease, coronary vasospasm/Prinzmetal angina, uncontrolled hypertension, stroke/TIA, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment) and withholds/modifies therapy if present.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing triptans (notably ergot derivatives within 24 hours; other triptans; MAO inhibitors for sumatriptan; serotonergic agents with counseling/monitoring for serotonin toxicity risk).

Performs a contraindication check before ordering/arranging oxygen therapy (e.g., significant CO2 retention risk in severe COPD/hypercapnic respiratory failure requiring individualized plan) and documents/communicates appropriate precautions if relevant.

Assesses for red flags/secondary headache features and indicates need for urgent evaluation/imaging if atypical presentation (new neurologic deficit, sudden thunderclap onset, systemic symptoms, immunosuppression, new onset after age >50, change in pattern).
Provides safety-net advice (seek urgent care for chest pain, severe shortness of breath, new neurologic symptoms, or worst-ever headache) and outlines follow-up during the cluster period.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose cluster headache, provide first-line acute therapy (oxygen + SC sumatriptan), and refer to neurology for preventive management and confirmation.
must_collect: attack frequency (up to 8/day) and circadian pattern
ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (tearing, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating)
current medications (ergots, MAOI, other triptans, serotonergic agents)
steps: Elicit headache phenotype consistent with cluster headache and assess for red flags.
Communicate diagnosis: cluster headache with associated autonomic symptoms; discuss familial tendency if present.
Complete required safety checks prior to prescribing: allergy, contraindications, and drug interactions (especially for triptans).
Prescribe acute therapy: high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather and subcutaneous sumatriptan with dosing limits and usage instructions.
Arrange neurology/headache specialist referral for preventive therapy planning (e.g., verapamil with ECG monitoring) and further evaluation if needed.
Provide safety-net instructions and follow-up plan during the cluster period.

acceptable_variants.description: Uses intranasal triptan instead of subcutaneous due to patient preference/needle aversion, while still prescribing oxygen and completing safety checks; refers to neurology.
steps: Diagnose cluster headache and explain autonomic symptoms.
Safety checks: allergy, contraindication, drug interaction review.
Prescribe oxygen plus intranasal zolmitriptan 5 mg (may repeat once after ≥2 hours; max 10 mg/24 h) or intranasal sumatriptan 20 mg (max 40 mg/24 h), with clear instructions.
Refer to neurology/headache specialist for preventive management and monitoring.

acceptable_variants.description: If triptan is contraindicated, provides oxygen as primary abortive therapy and refers urgently for alternative acute options and preventive initiation.
steps: Diagnose cluster headache and explain autonomic symptoms.
Identify triptan contraindication or high-risk interaction; document and avoid triptan prescribing.
Prescribe high-flow oxygen with appropriate instructions and precautions.
Refer to neurology/headache specialist (expedited) for alternative acute therapies and preventive strategy.
